title: practical english usage - Part 12
content:
she is already identified by the name Hannah Smith.) See also identifying (The relative clause, who tried to steal my cat, does not identify the person - thing is meant). Example: There's Hannah Smith, who tried to steal my cat.

any, anybody, etc are called 'non-affirmative' or non-assertive' forms. Other sentence they are often replaced by any, anybody, anywhere, etc. Words like somewhere, etc are used most often in affirmative sentences. modal auxiliary verb  one of the verbs can, could, may, might, must, will, shall, in careful writing because of the danger of misunderstanding.

Example: Looking out of misrelated participle (also called hanging or dangling participle)  a participle Example: I have never been to Africa.

mid-position  If an adverbial is in mid-position in a sentence, it is with the verb.

well; suddenly; fast; without any delay.

manner  an adverbial of manner describes how  something happens. Examples: will explain; has arrived; would have been forgotten.

between I, you, and he/she, or between am, are and is, are differences of person.

perfect infinitive  (to) have + past participle. Example: to have arrived.

I should/ would have agreed; He would have known.

perfect conditional  should/would have + past participle. Examples: I have forgotten; She had failed; having arrived; to have finished.

perfect  a verb form made with the auxiliary have + past participle. Examples: past simple  see simple past.

Examples: I was going; They were stopping.
